subject: Physics
unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: What is weight?

options.A: The amount of matter in an object
options.B: The gravitational force acting on an object
options.C: The resistance of an object to motion
options.D: The force needed to accelerate an object

questions.hint: Weight depends on gravity, not just mass.
questions.explanation: Weight is the magnitude of the gravitational force on an object: \(W = mg\). Option A is mass, not weight.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: Weight is the magnitude of the gravitational force acting on a body

explanations_tiered.definition: Weight is the gravitational force exerted on an object by a planet.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Weight = mass × gravitational acceleration. It varies with location, unlike mass.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Weight is how heavy something feels because of gravity pulling it down.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Weight \(W = mg\) is a force measured in newtons, not kilograms.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Weight = \(\vec{F}_g = m\vec{g}\). On Earth, \(g \approx 9.8\text{m/s}^2\), so \(W = mg\).
misconceptions: Confusing weight (force) with mass (kg). Weight changes on Moon; mass does not.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Weight = \(mg\) (force, N). Mass constant, weight varies.

glossary_entry.definition: Gravitational force on an object, \(W = mg\).

flashcard.back: The gravitational force on an object: \(W = mg\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Gravitational force on an object: \(W = mg\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise weight changes with g, mass does not.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Weight is gravitational force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A person pushes against a wall with a force of \(50\text{N}\). What force does the wall exert on the person?
questions.hint: Newton's third law: action and reaction are equal and opposite.
questions.explanation: Newton's third law states \(F_{12} = -F_{21}\). The wall exerts an equal and opposite force of \(50\text{N}\) on the person. Option A would violate the law.

calculation_steps: Newton's third law: action force = - reaction force

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

explanations_tiered.definition: Newton's third law: every action has an equal and opposite reaction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Forces come in pairs: \(F_{12} = -F_{21}\). Magnitudes equal, directions opposite, act on different objects.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Push a wall, it pushes you back just as hard — that's Newton's third law.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If person exerts \(50\text{N}\) on wall, wall exerts \(50\text{N}\) on person, opposite direction.
explanations_tiered.advanced: \(\vec{F}_{\text{person on wall}} = -\vec{F}_{\text{wall on person}}\). Magnitudes equal: \(50\text{N}\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify action force (50 N on wall).
Step 2: Apply Newton's third law: reaction equal in magnitude.
Step 3: Wall exerts 50 N on person.
misconceptions: Some think reaction is smaller or zero — false; always equal.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Newton III: equal and opposite forces, different objects.

glossary_entry.definition: For every action, an equal and opposite reaction.

flashcard.front: Push wall with 50 N. Force wall exerts on you?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 50 N push on wall. Wall's push on you?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Action and reaction are equal and opposite.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise forces act on different objects.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Newton's third law: equal and opposite force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: Which type of friction acts when an object is sliding across a surface?
questions.hint: Think about motion versus no motion.
questions.explanation: Kinetic friction (or sliding friction) opposes motion when surfaces slide past each other. Static friction acts before motion starts. Option A is for stationary objects.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: Kinetic friction: the friction between two contacting surfaces when one of them slides over the other

explanations_tiered.definition: Kinetic friction opposes motion between surfaces already sliding relative to each other.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Kinetic friction is usually less than maximum static friction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you slide a book across a table, kinetic friction slows it down.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Kinetic friction acts during sliding; static friction acts before sliding starts.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Kinetic friction force \(f_k = \mu_k N\), where \(\mu_k\) is coefficient of kinetic friction.
reasoning_steps: Identify if object is sliding (moving relative to surface).
If stationary (tending to move) → static friction.
misconceptions: Some think static friction acts during motion — incorrect; static friction is only before sliding.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Kinetic friction: sliding, usually smaller than static.

glossary_entry.definition: Friction between surfaces sliding past each other.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare kinetic vs static: kinetic is usually smaller.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Kinetic friction acts during sliding.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A force of \(40\text{N}\) is applied horizontally to push a box \(5\text{m}\) across a floor. How much work is done?
questions.hint: Work = force × displacement in direction of force.
questions.explanation: Work \(W = F_{\parallel}S = 40\text{N} \times 5\text{m} = 200\text{J}\). Option A results from dividing force by displacement.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: A boy pushed a box by a force of 60 N through 12 m

explanations_tiered.definition: Work = force × displacement when force and displacement are parallel.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Only the force component in the direction of displacement does work.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Push a box 5 m with 40 N force: work = 40 × 5 = 200 joules.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Work is a scalar: \(W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} = Fd\cos0^\circ = Fd = 200\text{J}\).
reasoning_steps: Identify force parallel to displacement (40 N).
misconceptions: Dividing force by displacement (40/5=8 J) is a common error.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy transferred by force causing displacement.

flashcard.front: 40 N pushes box 5 m. Work done?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Force × displacement in direction of force.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise force must be parallel to displacement.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A \(0.1\text{kg}\) ball has a kinetic energy of \(20\text{J}\). What is its speed?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy: \(E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \Rightarrow v = \sqrt{2E_k/m} = \sqrt{2 \times 20 / 0.1} = \sqrt{400} = 20\text{m/s}\). Option A forgets the ½ factor.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: A 200 g ball is thrown at a speed of 20 m/s. What is kinetic energy?

explanations_tiered.beginner: If a light ball has 20 J of motion energy, it must move fast.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Check unit conversion: mass in kg.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A motor does \(600\text{J}\) of work in \(10\text{s}\). What is its power?
questions.explanation: Power \(P = W/t = 600\text{J} / 10\text{s} = 60\text{W}\). Option A results from dividing time by work.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: If a car used up to 1500 J of energy in 5 seconds, power = 300 W

explanations_tiered.definition: Power = work done per unit time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Power measures how fast work is done: \(P = W/t\). Unit watt = J/s.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a machine does 600 joules of work in 10 seconds, its power is 60 watts.
misconceptions: Dividing time by work (10/600 ≈ 0.0167 W) is incorrect.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate power from work and time

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a contact force?
questions.hint: Contact forces require physical touch between objects.
questions.explanation: Frictional force arises from direct surface contact, making it a contact force. Magnetic, gravitational, and electrostatic forces act without touch (non-contact).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: Contact forces include applied forces, normal forces, frictional forces, and spring forces

explanations_tiered.definition: Contact forces require physical touch between objects.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Contact forces include friction, normal force, tension, and applied force.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Friction is a contact force because surfaces must touch to rub.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Frictional force requires direct surface contact, so it is a contact force.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Non-contact forces act via fields; contact forces act via direct mechanical interaction.
misconceptions: Some think gravity is contact — it acts at a distance.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Contact forces: friction, normal, applied, spring.

glossary_entry.definition: Force requiring physical contact between objects.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Friction is a contact force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: If the speed of a moving object is doubled, what happens to its kinetic energy?
questions.hint: Kinetic energy depends on speed squared.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy \(E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\). If \(v\) doubles, \(v^2\) quadruples, so kinetic energy quadruples. Option A confuses with momentum.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

explanations_tiered.definition: Kinetic energy scales with the square of speed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Doubling speed increases kinetic energy by factor of \(2^2 = 4\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you go twice as fast, you have four times the motion energy.
misconceptions: Doubling speed doubles kinetic energy (confusing with momentum).

glossary_entry.definition: Kinetic energy is proportional to speed squared.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. \(E_k \propto v^2\), so doubling v quadruples E_k.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A book is sliding across a table and gradually slows down. A student claims that a force is needed to keep it moving. Why is this claim incorrect?

options.A: The book naturally slows down without any force
options.B: Friction is the force that slows it down, not the absence of a pushing force
options.C: Newton's first law applies only to objects at rest
options.D: Slowing down means no net force acts

questions.hint: Think about what causes the book to stop.
questions.explanation: Newton's first law states an object in motion stays in motion without net force. Friction provides the net force that slows the book. Option C is false; the law applies to moving objects too.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: a body continues to be in its state of rest or uniform motion unless acted on by unbalanced force

explanations_tiered.definition: Newton's first law: no net force → constant velocity (including zero).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Slowing occurs because friction (net force) opposes motion, not because motion requires a sustaining force.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The book doesn't need a push to keep moving; friction is the force that stops it.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Friction is the unbalanced force causing deceleration. Without friction, the book would continue moving forever.
explanations_tiered.advanced: According to Newton's first law, \(\vec{v}\) constant if \(\sum \vec{F}=0\). Here friction \(\neq 0\), so velocity changes.
reasoning_steps: Identify the misconception: motion needs constant force.
Recall Newton's first law: motion continues without net force.
Slowing happens because friction (a force) opposes motion.
misconceptions: Ancient belief: constant motion requires constant push — Galileo/Newton disproved this.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: No net force → constant velocity. Slowing = net force (friction).

glossary_entry.definition: Tendency of an object to resist changes in motion.

flashcard.front: Why does a sliding book stop even without a push?
flashcard.back: Friction (net force) slows it; no push needed to keep moving.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does sliding book stop without a push?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Friction is the net force causing stopping; motion doesn't need sustaining force.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Motion continues without force; friction stops it.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Correct misconception about force and motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Friction slows the book; motion does not require constant force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A \(1000\text{kg}\) car accelerates at \(2\text{m/s}^2\). What net force acts on it?
questions.explanation: Net force \(F = ma = 1000\text{kg} \times 2\text{m/s}^2 = 2000\text{N}\). Option A divides mass by acceleration.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

explanations_tiered.definition: Newton's second law: net force = mass × acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: \(F = ma\) relates force, mass, and acceleration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A heavy car needs a bigger push to accelerate.
misconceptions: Dividing mass by acceleration (1000/2=500 N) is a common error.

flashcard.front: 1000 kg car, 2 m/s² acceleration. Net force?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students net force = ma, not mg.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate net force from mass and acceleration
